eviction.court_process_summary_rule:
{"statutes":"ORS §§ 90.392, 90.394, 90.398, 90.427, 90.445","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"4-day notice to pay or vacate for nonpayment (ORS 90.394, extended to 10 days by SR 890 effective 2025)","no_cause_termination":"30-day notice (90.427) for month-to-month; 90-day notice if tenant has lived there 1+ year","cause_termination":"14-day notice to cure or vacate for noncompliance (ORS 90.392)","immediate_termination":"24 hours notice for drug/alcohol violations, violent/criminal activity (ORS 90.396, 90.398)","retaliation_prohibited":"ORS 90.385"},"full_text":"ORS 90.427 No cause termination; notice; reasons prohibited. (1)(a) A landlord shall not terminate a tenancy without cause. The landlord must provide a written notice specifying the reason for termination. (b) If no specific cause exists, the landlord must provide a termination notice of at least 30 days for month-to-month tenancy. If the tenant has resided in the dwelling unit for one year or more, the landlord must provide at least 90 days' notice.
(2) A landlord may not terminate or refuse to renew a tenancy because: (a) The tenant has exercised any right or remedy under this chapter or other law; (b) The tenant has made a complaint to a governmental entity; or (c) The landlord's desire to convert the dwelling unit to another use, unless the landlord pays relocation assistance equal to one month's rent.
ORS 90.392 Termination for cause. If a tenant materially breaches the rental agreement or fails to comply with the obligations of tenancy, the landlord may terminate the rental agreement by giving not less than 14 days' written notice specifying the breach and stating that the rental agreement will terminate if the breach is not cured within 14 days. If the breach is not cured within the 14 days, the rental agreement terminates.
ORS 90.394 Termination for nonpayment of rent. If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may give the tenant a notice that the tenant has 4 days (extended to 10 days under SR 890 effective 2025) to pay the rent or vacate. If the tenant does not pay within the notice period, the landlord may proceed with termination."}

sources.title: Bootstrapped from app/data/state_laws.json

{"statutes":"ORS §§ 90.300, 90.302, 90.304","full_text":"ORS 90.300 Security deposit. (1) A landlord may not require a tenant to pay a security deposit in an amount or value that exceeds an amount equal to one month's rent, excluding any portion of the rent that is for utilities, if the landlord is furnishing or providing utilities at the beginning of the tenancy. (2) The landlord shall provide the tenant with a written checklist or statement recording the condition of the dwelling unit, including all furnishings and appliances, at the beginning of the tenancy. The tenant may supplement or rebut this checklist. (3) Upon termination of tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant within 31 days after the tenant has vacated the premises, if the landlord retains any portion of the deposit, the landlord shall give the tenant an itemized statement of the basis for and amount of any security deposit retained and return any remaining balance. (4) If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 31 days, the tenant may recover the amount of the deposit, regardless of whether there is any actual damage.\nORS 90.302 Permissible fees. A landlord may not charge a tenant any fee other than: (a) A security deposit not to exceed one month's rent; (b) Last month's rent if provided for in the rental agreement; (c) A charge for late payment of rent if the charge does not exceed 5 percent of the monthly rent; (d) A charge for anreturned check if the landlord has previously charged a fee for a returned check or if the charge is made pursuant to a written agreement; (e) A fee authorized by ORS 90.295 (screening fee)."}
extractedQuotes: Maximum amount: may not exceed 5

sources.title:
{"statutes":"ORS §§ 90.392, 90.394, 90.398, 90.427, 90.445","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"4-day notice to pay or vacate for nonpayment (ORS 90.394, extended to 10 days by SR 890 effective 2025)","no_cause_termination":"30-day notice (90.427) for month-to-month; 90-day notice if tenant has lived there 1+ year","cause_termination":"14-day notice to cure or vacate for noncompliance (ORS 90.392)","immediate_termination":"24 hours notice for drug/alcohol violations, violent/criminal activity (ORS 90.396, 90.398)","retaliation_prohibited":"ORS 90.385"},"full_text":"ORS 90.427 No cause termination; notice; reasons prohibited. (1)(a) A landlord shall not terminate a tenancy without cause. The landlord must provide a written notice specifying the reason for termination. (b) If no specific cause exists, the landlord must provide a termination notice of at least 30 days for month-to-month tenancy. If the tenant has resided in the dwelling unit for one year or more, the landlord must provide at least 90 days' notice.
(2) A landlord may not terminate or refuse to renew a tenancy because: (a) The tenant has exercised any right or remedy under this chapter or other law; (b) The tenant has made a complaint to a governmental entity; or (c) The landlord's desire to convert the dwelling unit to another use, unless the landlord pays relocation assistance equal to one month's rent.
ORS 90.392 Termination for cause. If a tenant materially breaches the rental agreement or fails to comply with the obligations of tenancy, the landlord may terminate the rental agreement by giving not less than 14 days' written notice specifying the breach and stating that the rental agreement will terminate if the breach is not cured within 14 days. If the breach is not cured within the 14 days, the rental agreement terminates.
ORS 90.394 Termination for nonpayment of rent. If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may give the tenant a notice that the tenant has 4 days (extended to 10 days under SR 890 effective 2025) to pay the rent or vacate. If the tenant does not pay within the notice period, the landlord may proceed with termination."}

If a tenant materially breaches the rental agreement or fails to comply with the obligations of tenancy, the landlord may terminate the rental agreement by giving not less than 14 days' written notice specifying the breach and stating that the rental agreement will terminate if the breach is not cured within 14 days. If the breach is not cured within the 14 days, the rental agreement terminates.
ORS 90.394 Termination for nonpayment of rent. If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may give the tenant a notice that the tenant has 4 days (extended to 10 days under SR 890 effective 2025) to pay the rent or vacate. If the tenant does not pay within the notice period, the landlord may proceed with termination."}

If a tenant materially breaches the rental agreement or fails to comply with the obligations of tenancy, the landlord may terminate the rental agreement by giving not less than 14 days' written notice specifying the breach and stating that the rental agreement will terminate if the breach is not cured within 14 days. If the breach is not cured within the 14 days, the rental agreement terminates.
ORS 90.394 Termination for nonpayment of rent. If the tenant fails to pay rent when due, the landlord may give the tenant a notice that the tenant has 4 days (extended to 10 days under SR 890 effective 2025) to pay the rent or vacate. If the tenant does not pay within the notice period, the landlord may proceed with termination."}

sources.title:
{"statutes":"ORS § 90.322","full_text":"ORS 90.322 Landlord or agent access to dwelling unit. (1) A landlord may enter a dwelling unit only: (a) At a reasonable time; (b) In a reasonable manner; and (c) After proper notice to the tenant, except in emergencies.
(2) Proper notice means: (a) Verbal notice given at the time of entry, if the entry is at a reasonable time; or (b) Written notice left on the dwelling unit at least 24 hours before entry, if the entry is not at a reasonable time.
(3) The landlord may enter without notice in an emergency. (4) The tenant may request that the landlord not enter at certain times, if the request is reasonable and the tenant gives the landlord written notice.
(5) If the landlord makes an unlawful entry or a lawful entry in an unreasonable manner, the tenant may recover actual damages or $250, whichever is greater, and reasonable attorney fees."}

sources.title: {"statutes":"ORS §§ 90.320, 90.365, 90.368","full_text":"ORS 90.320 Landlord's duty to maintain dwelling unit in habitable condition. (1) A landlord shall maintain a dwelling unit in a habitable condition and shall: (a) Comply with all applicable building and housing codes; (b) Make all repairs and do whatever is necessary to put and keep the premises in a safe and habitable condition; (c) Keep all common areas of the premises clean and safe; (d) Maintain in good working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilation and air conditioning systems and appliances provided by the landlord; (e) Provide and maintain appropriate garbage containers and arrange for garbage removal if the landlord is responsible for removal; (f) Supply running water and reasonable hot water at all times.\n(2) The landlord is not responsible for conditions caused by the tenant or the tenant's family or guests. (3) If the landlord fails to comply with the obligations of this section, the tenant may: (a) Repair the condition and deduct the cost from rent; (b) Terminate the rental agreement; or (c) Recover actual damages, including damages for diminished rental value."}

(1) A landlord shall maintain a dwelling unit in a habitable condition and shall: (a) Comply with all applicable building and housing codes; (b) Make all repairs and do whatever is necessary to put and keep the premises in a safe and habitable condition; (c) Keep all common areas of the premises clean and safe; (d) Maintain in good working order all electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilation and air conditioning systems and appliances provided by the landlord; (e) Provide and maintain appropriate garbage containers and arrange for garbage removal if the landlord is responsible for removal; (f) Supply running water and reasonable hot water at all times.\n(2) The landlord is not responsible for conditions caused by the tenant or the tenant's family or guests. (3) If the landlord fails to comply with the obligations of this section, the tenant may: (a) Repair the condition and deduct the cost from rent; (b) Terminate the rental agreement; or (c) Recover actual damages, including damages for diminished rental value."}
